has been to propose a new technique with which to predict the observable signal .

present data already give interesting constraints on the skewness parameter [x1] , and over the next several years we expect that the _ planck _ survey satellite will make these constraints very stringent . it is even possible that higher - order moments , such as the kurtosis parameter [x176] [ref] will become better constrained [ref] . to meet the need of the observational community for comparison with theory , reliable estimates of these non - linear quantities will be necessary for various models of early - universe physics .

gaussianity . in comparison , our proposed moment transport method exhibits several clear differences .

first , the conventional method functions best when we base the [x0] expansion on a flat hypersurface immediately after horizon exit . in our method , we make the opposite choice and move the flat hypersurface as close as possible to the time of observation . after this , the role of the [x0] formula is to provide no more than the non - linear gauge transformation between field fluctuations and the curvature perturbation .

from the second - order part of the gauge transformation from [x180] to [x2] .

the contribution from intrinsic non - linearities of the field fluctuations , measured by the skewnesses [x40] , is negligible . in such cases

one can obtain a useful formula for [x1] by approximating the field distribution as an exact gaussian .

the non - gaussianity produced in such cases arises from a distortion of comoving hypersurfaces with respect to adjacent spatially flat hypersurfaces .

pureSurvivors.text:
gaussianity produced by super - horizon evolution in two - field models of inflation .

our method is based on the real - space distribution of inflationary field values on a flat hypersurface , which can be thought of as a probability density function whose evolution is determined by a form of the collisionless boltzmann equation . using a cumulant representation [ref] to expand our density function around an exact gaussian

, we derive ordinary differential equations which evolve the moments of this distribution .

gaussian , we derive ordinary differential equations which evolve the moments of this distribution .

further , we show how these moments are related to observable quantities , such as the dimensionless bispectrum measured by [x1] [ref] .

we present numerical results which show that this method gives good agreement with other techniques .

it is not necessary to make any assumptions about the inflationary model beyond requiring a canonical kinetic term and applying the slow - roll approximation .

other is a gauge transformation from field fluctuations to the curvature peturbation , [x2] .

it would be difficult or impossible to observe this split within the context of other calculational schemes , such as the conventional [x0] formalism .

a second advantage of our method is connected with the computational cost of numerical implementation .

analytic formulas for [x1] are known in certain cases , mostly in the context of the [x0] framework , but only for very specific choices of the potential [ref] or hubble rate [ref] .

but only for very specific choices of the potential [ref] or hubble rate [ref] .

these formulas become increasingly cumbersome as the number of fields increases , or if one studies higher moments [ref] . in the future , it seems clear that studies of complex models with many fields will increasingly rely on numerical methods .

the numerical [x0] framework requires the solution to a number of ordinary differential equations which scales exponentially with the number of fields .

since some models include hundreds of fields , this may present a significant obstacle [ref] .

has been used by lehners & renaux - petel to obtain numerical results [ref] .

another numerical scheme has been introduced by huston & malik [ref] .

what properties do we require of a successful prediction ? consider a typical observer , drawn at random from an ensemble of realizations of inflation . in any of the formalisms

discussed above , we aim to estimate the statistical properties of the curvature perturbation which would be measured by such an observer .
